policy_statement: Integrate community resilience and climate justice criteria into department-level strategic planning and annual budget setting by 2023.
mechanism_description: Integrating resilience and climate justice criteria into departmental strategic planning and annual budget setting helps guide standard practices and spending decisions toward supporting community resilience and equitable climate outcomes.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance mainstreaming → ↑ institutional adaptive capacity → ↓ exposure/vulnerability to climate hazards
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: City departments embed resilience and climate justice criteria into standard strategic planning cycles and annual budget formulation, shifting how priorities are set and how funds are allocated.  
2) Mediating process: Institutionalization of risk screening, equity impact consideration, cross-department coordination, and dedicated/consistent resourcing improves the city’s ability to anticipate, plan for, and manage climate risks (organizational learning + procedural controls).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: More resilient and equitable services/infrastructure and better-targeted investments lead to reduced harm from climate extremes (e.g., heat, flooding) especially for vulnerable communities—an adaptation benefit via increased adaptive capacity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved preparedness and reduced vulnerability to climate hazards through stronger, equity-centered institutional planning and budgeting (i.e., increased adaptive capacity).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Mainstreaming resilience/justice into planning and budgeting chiefly improves governance capacity to anticipate and manage climate hazards, which reduces vulnerability (adaptation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While such mainstreaming can indirectly enable mitigation or resource-efficiency investments, the mechanism itself is about decision-process changes that increase preparedness and equitable resilience outcomes rather than directly reducing GHGs or resource inputs.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific implementation mandates primarily emissions-focused budget criteria (e.g., carbon budgets) the dominant pathway could shift toward mitigation; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly centers resilience and justice, keeping adaptation dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that integrates resilience/climate justice into routine planning and budgeting is principally building institutional adaptive capacity, regardless of sector or specific hazard focus.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy secondarily supports mitigation, nature-based solutions, or resource efficiency depends on the particular funded actions and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could straddle mitigation if “climate criteria” are operationalized mainly as emissions/carbon-budget requirements; could also overlap with “just transition” governance. Under the stated resilience + climate justice framing, adaptation via adaptive-capacity building remains dominant.
key_indicators: "Integrate community resilience and climate justice criteria"; "department-level strategic planning"; "annual budget setting"; "departments will begin to intentionally consider how standard practices can be used to support or impair community resilience and climate justice"; "by 2023"
co_benefits: Improved equity and procedural justice in budgeting and planning; better general risk management and service delivery effectiveness (beyond climate-specific hazards).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a regulatory mandate (an internal requirement), but the core mechanism is integration into planning/budget processes, so planning-strategy is the best fit. No specific funding commitment or disclosure/reporting requirement is stated in the text.
